key_insight: A viscoplastic-damage finite element model can predict fracture in 3D-printed polymer TPMS lattices by coupling stored elastic energy and plastic strain to damage initiation.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable in silico design of lightweight lattice structures with improved toughness and failure prediction under tension, compression, and non-monotonic loading.
method: Implemented a high-fidelity rate-dependent viscoplastic constitutive model in Abaqus/Explicit via UMAT.
Added a damage initiation criterion for amorphous polymers based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
Used a gradient-damage framework to simulate fracture evolution and structural failure.
evidence_signal: The model was validated against experimental results for a unit cell under non-monotonic loading and for lattices under tension, showing quantitative and qualitative agreement.
watch_item: The approach depends on careful calibration of damage parameters and may be sensitive to mesh regularization and constitutive assumptions for different polymer materials.
paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
